topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the faded ink on the antique ledger, the paper brittle beneath his fingertips. The air in the dusty attic hung thick and still, mirroring the heavy quiet settling in his chest. He’d stumbled upon it while clearing out his grandmother’s estate—a meticulously detailed, chronological account of his life. As he read the entries, a cold dread began to seep into his bones. The milestones, the triumphs, the very trajectory of his existence were all there, but the voice behind the pen belonged to someone else. A strange, hollow ache settled behind his eyes, making the world seem muted, as if viewed through a pane of frosted glass. He leaned back against a stack of forgotten trunks, his gaze fixed on the script, unable to summon the energy to move anything else.

[story 2]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating monotony. Sarah stared blankly at the screen, the words scrolling across the monitor blurring into meaningless shapes. It was a document she’d unearthed during a routine archival review—her own life story, written in meticulous detail, yet utterly alien. Every achievement, every whispered memory, felt like a performance she hadn't auditioned for. A profound inertia gripped her; her fingers hovered over the mouse, unwilling to click, unwilling to engage. A slow, heavy sigh escaped her lips, a sound devoid of real breath, as she absorbed the chilling realization that the narrative of her life had been constructed by an unseen hand.

[story 3]

Marcus sat by the window, the city lights twinkling with a distant, indifferent brilliance. He held the bound manuscript, the weight of it pressing down on his hands. The prose was flawless, emotionally resonant, yet every pivotal moment felt like a carefully curated stage direction rather than lived experience. He ran a hand over his face, feeling the dull throb behind his temples. There was no spark, no desire to dissect the text further, only a profound sense of displacement. He felt anchored, yet utterly unmoored, watching the reflections in the glass—a vacant stare that spoke volumes about the emptiness where his own story should have been.

He’d stumbled upon it while clearing out his grandmother’s estate—a meticulously detailed, chronological account of his life. As he read the entries, a cold dread began to seep into his bones. The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating monotony. Sarah stared blankly at the screen, the words scrolling across the monitor blurring into meaningless shapes. It was a document she’d unearthed during a routine archival review—her own life story, written in meticulous detail, yet utterly alien. Every achievement, every whispered memory, felt like a performance she hadn't auditioned for. A profound inertia gripped her; her fingers hovered over the mouse, unwilling to click, unwilling to engage. A slow, heavy sigh escaped her lips, a sound devoid of real breath, as she absorbed the chilling realization that the narrative of her life had been constructed by an unseen hand.

Marcus sat by the window, the city lights twinkling with a distant, indifferent brilliance. He held the bound manuscript, the weight of it pressing down on his hands. The prose was flawless, emotionally resonant, yet every pivotal moment felt like a carefully curated stage direction rather than lived experience. He ran a hand over his face, feeling the dull throb behind his temples. There was no spark, no desire to dissect the text further, only a profound sense of displacement. He felt anchored, yet utterly unmoored, watching the reflections in the glass—a vacant stare that spoke volumes about the emptiness where his own story should have been.
